coredata.dc:title: Ligand engineering with heterocyclic aromatic thiol doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Anthony, Ancy Milrad
Pandurangan, Prabhu
Abbas, Sohrab
coredata.dc:description: Insights on the origin of photoluminescence (PL) in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are useful to explore its application in the technological and biomedical fields. Herein, we demonstrate the use of three heterocyclic aromatic thiol ligands (namely, 2-mercaptobenzimidazole (MBI), 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT) and 2-mercaptobenzoxazole (MBO)) doped CQDs (CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO) to address the significance of heteroatoms in the optical and electrochemical characteristics of CQDs. Interestingly, we found that nitrogen moieties played a vital role in tailoring the optical and electrochemical properties of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. Owing to the enriched nitrogen moieties and less surface trap states in CQDs-MBI, which exhibits superior quantum yield (QY) and lifetime than CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. Moreover, for the CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT we achieved two sets of emission whereas for the CQDs-MBO attained three sets, also all these CQDs display both excitation independent and dependent emission behavior due to the distinct surface states which is peculiar to existing literature reports. To substantiate the importance of ligand engineering with these three different CQDs, we further investigated the electrochemical characteristics as well. These tunable optical and electrochemical properties of the three different CQDs suggest great scope in the development of new luminescent and sensor materials.

head.ce:title: Ligand engineering with heterocyclic aromatic thiol doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, School of Chemical Science, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai, 600 025, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Solid-state Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai, 400 085, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Solid-state Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai - 400 085, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Insights on the origin of photoluminescence (PL) in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are useful to explore its application in the technological and biomedical fields. Herein, we demonstrate the use of three heterocyclic aromatic thiol ligands (namely, 2-mercaptobenzimidazole (MBI), 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT) and 2-mercaptobenzoxazole (MBO)) doped CQDs (CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO) to address the significance of heteroatoms in the optical and electrochemical characteristics of CQDs. Interestingly, we found that nitrogen moieties played a vital role in tailoring the optical and electrochemical properties of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. Owing to the enriched nitrogen moieties and less surface trap states in CQDs-MBI, which exhibits superior quantum yield (QY) and lifetime than CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. Moreover, for the CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT we achieved two sets of emission whereas for the CQDs-MBO attained three sets, also all these CQDs display both excitation independent and dependent emission behavior due to the distinct surface states which is peculiar to existing literature reports. To substantiate the importance of ligand engineering with these three different CQDs, we further investigated the electrochemical characteristics as well. These tunable optical and electrochemical properties of the three different CQDs suggest great scope in the development of new luminescent and sensor materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an emerging class of zero dimensional, heavy metal free and low toxic fluorescent carbon nanomaterials that exists in the size range of ∼2–10 nm and also consist of sp2 hybridized carbon constructed core bounded by amorphous shells with abundant functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl and epoxy moieties [1–6]. Ligand engineering played an excellent role in the structure and optical properties of CQDs. In most of the previous literature reports ligand engineering in CQDs was achieved by heteroatom doping using different ligands which improve the optical and electronic properties of CQDs by creating n-type or p-type carriers [2,7–11]. Heteroatom doping in CQDs signifies a multifaceted and potent technique which is used to further ameliorate the morphological features and chemical composition of CQDs. There are two types of heteroatoms doping available that depend on the number of heteroatoms doped into the CQDs i.e., single atom doped (e.g. nitrogen [12], sulfur [13], oxygen [14], boron [15], phosphorous [16], etc.) and multiplex atoms co-doped (e.g. N/S [14], N/P [17], N/B [18], etc.). Among these, multiplex heteroatom doping is unique due to the synergistic effect of doped hetero atoms [2]. Especially, N/S co-doped CQDs have been energetically scrutinized by many researchers due to their unique photoluminescence (PL) property by inducing more active sites into the CQDs. Here the atomic radius of the heteroatom nitrogen is near to carbon and electronegativity of sulfur is analogous to carbon. These similarity behavior of the heteroatoms are the major reason for the low toxicity, improved PL and broad range of applications [2,8]. Moreover the nitrogen atom in the surface of CQDs will be able to trap more electrons which facilitates the enhanced radiative recombination. Likewise the sulfur atom enriches the density of nitrogen atom and further improves the radiative recombination results enhancement in the PL property of CQDs [8,14,19–21].

N/S co-doped CQDs was first synthesized by Dong et al., in 2013 [14], via one step hydrothermal treatment using citric acid as carbon source and l-cysteine as both N and S source. The achieved N/S co-doped CQDs have excitation independent emission with high fluorescent quantum yield due to the synergistic effect of doped nitrogen and sulfur atom. Followed by them several research groups’ synthesis N/S co-doped CQDs by using various ligands as N and S source. For example, in 2014 Ding et al. [22], synthesized blue luminescent N/S co-doped CQDs via hydrothermal route using α-lipoic acid and ethylenediamine, which exhibited excellent PL properties and low cytotoxicity. Owing to these better properties which could be effectively applied to the detection of Fe3+ ion for practical applications and multicolor cell imaging. Recently in 2021, Jin et al. [25] synthesized red emissive N/S co-doped CQDs using polythiophene via chemical cutting by nitric acid and they revealed that the red emission was mainly arising from the sp2 domain then they apply the doped red emissive CQDs for in-vitro imaging. Similarly, several researchers use various ligands to synthesis N/S co-doped CQDs [2,8,23,24,26].

Here in we use three different heterocyclic aromatic thiolated ligands for the synthesis of heteroatom co-doped CQDs i.e., 2-mercaptobenzimidazole (MBI), 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT) and 2-mercaptobenzoxazole (MBO). These three CQDs exhibit significant role in biomedical and industrial fields due to their distinctive properties [27]. These thiolates are multifunctional and diverge only in the heteroatoms existing in the five membered ring. Owing to this difference in the structure when we functionalize the CQDs with these heterocyclic thiolated ligands, their properties get altered depending upon the doping ratio and nature of heteroatoms. In this work we investigated the role of the heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen in the morphological, optical and electrochemical performances of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO.

Spectra-pure graphite powder, sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), nitric acid (HNO3, 69%) hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) and ethanol were purchased from SRL Chemicals. The heterocyclic thiolates such as 2-Mercaptobenzimidazole (MBI), 2-Mercaptobenzoxazole (MBO) and 2-Mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Particle size of as-synthesized heteroatom functionalized CQDs were found using HR-TEM (JOEL Japan, JEM-2100 Plus) and DLS, Horiba SZ-100. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a PHI 5000 Versa Probe III surface analysis system. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using Bruker instrument in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer, Hitachi-3500 and the photoluminescence spectra were recorded using Fluoromax Plus Spectrofluorimeter. Fluorescence life-time measurements were carried out using Time Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC), Fluorocube life-time system. Zeta potential measurement was carried out using Horiba SZ-100. The samples were excited using 460 nm LED. The electrochemical experiments were carried out using PARSTAT electrochemical workstation (Model 3000, USA) equipped with standard three electrode configuration. A cell assembly of volume 100 ml with the heterocyclic aromatic thiolated CQDs modified electrode was used as the working electrode and platinum and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as auxiliary and reference electrodes respectively. The supporting electrolyte potassium chloride (KCl), the redox probe K3Fe(CN)6 and l-Ascorbic Acid (AA) used for electrochemical experiments were purchased from SRL chemicals.

The heteroatom doped CQDs was synthesized by multistep synthesis method. Initially, we prepared graphite oxide from graphite powder and then CQDs from graphite oxide, followed by post-modification of CQDs for heteroatom doping as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphite oxide using graphite powder
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite oxide was synthesized using spectra-pure graphite powder as precursor according to Hummer's method [28]. In brief, 0.5 g of NaNO3 and 23 ml of H2SO4 was taken in a 500 ml beaker and was stirred in an ice-bath. After 5 min to the above mixture 1 g of graphite powder was slowly added and the mixture was again stirred for about 30 min in an ice-bath. Then 3 g of KMnO4 was added very slowly and stirred for about 2 h (at 20 °C). Then the temperature was raised to 35 °C yet again stirred for about 30 min. Now the mixture becomes pasty with brownish grey color. To this we added 46 ml of DDW (double distilled water), which caused violet effervescence. Subsequently, the reaction temperature was increased to 98 °C and after 15 min, 140 ml of warm water was added to the reaction mixture and the solution turned brown in color. To that we added 30% H2O2 till the formation of dark brownish yellow color. Finally, we got graphite oxide as dark brown filter cake and was filtered and washed with warm water for five times and the dark brown color precipitate obtained was dried in oven (50 °C). Then the obtained graphite oxide was stored in air tight container to prepare CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bare-CQDs from graphite oxide
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite oxide (1 g) was taken in a 250 ml round bottom flask (RBF), to this HNO3 (5 M, 100 ml) was added and the mixture was refluxed in an oil bath at 150 °C for 12 h. The attained yellow color supernatant liquid and brown color precipitate was separated by centrifugation at 6,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant liquid was neutralized with sodium hydroxide and was filtered using 0.2 μm PTFE filter and subjected to dialysis. The purified bare CQDs were kept at room temperature for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs-MBO, CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT from bare CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesis MBI functionalized CQDs, 0.05 g of bare CQDs was taken in a 100 ml RBF and was well dissolved in 50 ml of ethanol, to this 150 mg of MBI was added and stirred well for 2 h at ambient temperature. The achieved CQDs-MBI was subjected to further purifications using high speed centrifugation followed by effective filtration with 0.2 μm PTFE filter. The resultant CQDs-MBI was used for further investigation. Similar procedure was followed for the synthesis of CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs modified electrodes for AA sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
As per the reported literature [29], the paraffin wax impregnated graphite electrode (PIGE) was prepared, earlier to the fabrication of modified electrodes. Concisely, the graphite rod of about 4 cm length was submerged in molten paraffin wax to completely remove the air bubbles and get rid of from the paraffin wax before it get solidifies and kept back in room temperature for drying. Subsequently, the surface of PIGE was well smoothed/polished using the grit/tissue paper before modification by mechanical immobilization. For mechanical immobilization, the attained three CQDs in liquid phase are allowed for drying in hot air oven at 40
°C
to get powder. About 5 mg of CQDs-MBI was kept on a glass plate and the prepared PIGE was consistently pressed and rubbed over the CQDs-MBI in the glass plate. Afterwards, to remove the weakly adsorbed samples on the surface of the modified electrode, washed the surface with double distilled water and kept it for drying to use as a working electrode. Similar procedure was followed for the fabrication of CQDs-MBT, CQDs-MBO and bare CQDs modified electrodes.

Multistep synthesis approach was applied for the synthesis of the heteroatom doped CQDs and which follow three steps as shown in Scheme S1. Firstly via Hummer's method graphite oxide was prepared from graphite powder. Here, NaNO3 and KMnO4 were acting as catalysts especially, NaNO3 enhanced the basal plane oxidation and crystallite size reduction of graphite [30]. H2O2 was employed to terminate the oxidation process. The graphite oxide attained here was dark brown in color. Secondly, the graphite oxide obtained was subjected to nitric acid treatment. By this treatment the tiny quantum dots were prepared from the bulk carbon aggregates and which induced photoluminescence property of the bare CQDs. Finally as achieved bare CQDs was treated with the three structurally related heterocyclic aromatic ligands such as MBO, MBI and MBT. The pictorial representation of these ligands were displayed in Scheme S2. They diverged only in the heteroatoms existing in it. Interestingly, each of these three ligands were having three heteroatoms. Amongst MBI had one exocyclic sulfur and two cyclic nitrogen atoms. Conversely, MBT had one exocyclic sulfur, one cyclic nitrogen and one cyclic sulfur. However, MBO have three heteroatoms such as one exocyclic sulfur, one cyclic oxygen and one cyclic nitrogen. These heteroatoms present in these ligands performed major role when doping with bare CQDs. Depending upon the nature of ligand we used the physicochemical, optical as well as electrochemical properties also get altered and was described below.

The morphological features of as-synthesized MBI, MBT and MBO doped CQDs were analyzed by HR-TEM and was shown in Fig. 1 . Here, Fig. 1a shows the HR-TEM image of N/S-doped CQDs (CQDs-MBI) confirms the spherical shape and insets reveal the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern which depicts the amorphous nature of CQDs-MBI. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 1b) of CQDs-MBI exposes the mean particle size of about 4.7 nm. Fig. 1c depicts the HR-TEM image of CQDs-MBT displays that few dots are combined to form the aggregated/agglomerated oval shape with large size of about ∼47 nm (Fig. 1d) and the inset portrays the amorphous nature of CQDs-MBT. Here in CQDs-MBT, have two sulfur atoms and single nitrogen. In general the covalent radius of sulfur atom is almost 57% superior to oxygen and 49% larger than nitrogen. [31]. Owing to the larger radius and lowest bond enthalpy of sulfur atoms it is difficult to form stronger bonds, because the orbital overlapping of C–S bonds is very weak and easy to break and is described in more detail later. Thus the particle size of CQDs-MBT appears larger in size than CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBO. Fig. 1e shows the HR-TEM image of CQDs-MBO with quasi-spherical dots. Besides in insets SAED pattern suggested the crystalline nature of CQDs-MBO which was supported by lattice fringes attained. The d-spacing of 0.34 nm which displayed the hexagonal crystalline structures with (002) plane and 0.21 nm confirmed the presence of (100) planes of sp2 graphitic carbon core of CQDs-MBO, respectively [32,33]. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 1f) of CQDs-MBO reveals the average particle size of about 3.3 nm.

The average particle size distribution of heteroatoms doped CQDs were also analyzed using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and was shown in Fig. S1. The hydrodynamic diameter attained for CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO was 15 nm, 53 nm and 3.5 nm, respectively. The mean particle size trend observed from DLS was correlated with the trend observed from HR-TEM analysis for CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO.

The elemental composition and functional groups in the heteroatoms doped CQDs were confirmed by FT-IR spectral measurement. Fig. 2 a shows that the FT-IR spectra of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs MBO, respectively. The broad vibrational band in the range of 3600 - 3100 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. The characteristic strong peaks at 1617, 1590, 1409 and 1010 cm−1 could be assigned to the CO, CC (unsaturated bonds present in the carbon core), C–N stretching and C–O stretching respectively [34]. The narrow vibrational band near 1170 cm−1 suggests the presence of SO asymmetrical stretch [35], which confirms the formation of SOx (x = 2, 3, 4) bond during doping. The vibrational band at 739 cm−1 was assigned to the presence of C–S stretching and aromatic C–H bending vibrations. Stimulatingly, the absence of band in the range 2600–2550 cm−1 indicating the surface modification of CQDs also occurs by the thiol (-SH) group in MBI, MBT and MBO [36,37].

The nature of bonds and chemical composition of hetero atom doped CQDs was attained from the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The qualitative information about the sample was obtained from the survey spectrum. Fig. 2b shows the XPS survey spectrum of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO displays five peaks with the binding energies of 531, 400.4, 284.8, 229.4 and 165.5 eV representing the presence of O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s and S2p, respectively [38]. From this survey spectrum we observed that in CQDs-MBI doping of N/S confirmed by the greater intensities of N1s and S2p. In CQDs-MBT also N/S doping happened but the N1s peak was slightly diminished as compared to the CQDs-MBI, which indicated the enhanced sulfur moieties than nitrogen in CQDs-MBT and here the doping ratio of N:S is 1:3. But in CQDs-MBO the intensity of N1s was even reduced indicated that the presence of oxygen moieties almost replaced the nitrogen moieties. The comparative high resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of the three heteroatom doped CQDs (CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO) were shown in Fig. 3 a, which was deconvoluted into four peaks corresponding to C–C, (C–O, C–N/C–S), CO and O–CO (carboxyl) [39,40]. Here we can observe that the (C–O, C–N/C–S) bonds of CQDs-MBO moved towards higher binding energy (285.6 eV) as compared to others (285.1 eV) established that due to the higher electronegativity of MBO in CQDs-MBO, decreased the electron density around the surface of CQDs and effectively doped to the surface of CQDs through the three heteroatoms such as O [H-bonding], S and N. In the case of CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT were doped effectively through the two hetero atoms such as N and S. The O 1s spectra in Fig. 3b was fitted with two diverse peaks representing the presence of CO and C–OH. Here also CQDs-MBO showed better binding energy values than the other two. Interestingly the two deconvoluted peaks in CQDs-MBO constrained to have an approximately 1:1.5 (CO: C–OH) area ratio plus higher binding energy value of C–OH suggested that in addition to hydroxyl moieties in CQDs-MBO, the effective intermolecular hydrogen bonding also formed between the bare CQDs and MBO through the heteroatom oxygen. Similarly, CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT also had oxygen functionalities on the surface. The high resolution N1s spectra in Fig. 3c can be resolved into two discrete peaks such as pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen [41]. Here the pyrrolic nitrogen occupied the 90% of total peak area confirms the effective nitrogen doping occurred in CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. As observed in the survey spectrum CQDs-MBI had enriched nitrogen content than the others (CQDs-MBT & CQDs-MBO). The order of nitrogen moieties existing in these CQDs are CQDs-MBI (45.56%) > CQDs-MBT (31.68%) > CQDs-MBO (22.75%). Likewise high resolution S2p spectra in Fig. 3d describes the presence of C–S–C (S2p3/2), C–S–C (S2p1/2) and C-SOx (x = 2, 3 & 4) bonds [14,38,41], this authenticates the existence of sulfur doping in CQDs-MBO, CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT. Especially, CQDs-MBT had superior sulfur moieties than CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBO, which was proved by the highest binding energy of C–S–C (S2p3/2 & S2p1/2) bonds of CQDs-MBT. The order of sulfur moieties present in these CQDs are CQDs-MBT (59.55%) > CQDs-MBI (21.64%) > CQDs-MBO (18.79%). From the above results we can conclude that the obtained XPS results displayed satisfactory agreement with FT-IR analysis, obviously indicating the N, S- co doping happened in both CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT, besides O, N, S- doping occurred in CQDs-MBO.

From the FT-IR and XPS characterizations we come to know that due to the influence of the three ligands the surface of the three distinct CQDs such as CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO are surrounded by C–N, C–S, C–O, CO, CC, N–H and C–OH bonds. Among this CQDs-MBI have enriched nitrogen moieties, CQDs-MBT have enhanced sulfur moieties and CQDs-MBO have more oxygen moieties over the surface. When we compare the C–N, C–S and C–O bonds alone the order of covalent radius is, 1.05
Å
(sulfur) > 0.7
Å
(nitrogen) > 0.66
Å
oxygen. Owing to the larger covalent radius sulfur forms only weaker bonds. Also in the case of C–S bonds due to the large variation in size poor overlapping of orbitals occur causing weak interaction. While comparing the bond energies of C–N, C–S and C–O bonds the order is, C–O (358 kJ/mol) > C–N (293 kJ/mol) > C–S (259 kJ/mol). [31,42]. Thus due to the above reasons we conclude that the enhanced sulfur moieties over the surface of CQDs-MBT have large size than CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBO.

The most exciting feature of CQDs in application perspective is their variable optical properties with respect to the different chemical environment. The UV–Visible absorption spectra of the as-synthesized CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO were shown in Fig. 4 . The UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CQDs-MBI exhibited two absorption peaks at 297 nm and ∼365 nm that could be assigned to the n-π* transition of CO/CN bonds in the surface defect states induced by the heteroatoms [38]. Comparably, the UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CQDs-MBT shows three peaks at 252 nm, 297 nm and 324 nm. Amongst the absorption peak at 252 nm aroused due to the π-π* transition that occurred in the aromatic carbon core and the latter two peaks at 297 nm and 324 nm are stimulated by the n-π* transition of CO/CN bonds. Interestingly, CQDs-MBO depicted three absorption peaks at 335 nm, 432 nm and 503 nm. Amongst the absorption peak at 335 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of CO/CN bonds. The absorption peak at 432 nm was attributed to the presence of CN/CS conjugated bonds and the peak at 503 nm ascribed to the n-π* transition of CS bonds [38] suggesting the O, S, N-doping occurred in CQDs-MBO. The PL excitation (PLE) spectra of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO are shown in Fig. S2 and suggested that every emission was excited by an individual excitation band.

The photoluminescence spectra of CQDs-MBI were shown in Fig. 5 a. In this, the emission maximum (λ emi) of CQDs-MBI was observed at 523 nm when excited at 450 nm with stokes shift of about ∼73 nm, as obtained CQDs-MBI was yellowish and transparent under day light but exhibited bright greenish yellow (lime) emission under UV light (Inset 5a). The bright yellow emission aroused mainly due to the existence of various surface states on the surface of CQDs-MBI due to N/S doping which facilitate the radiative recombination and thereby enhanced the photoluminescence property. The normalized emission spectra of CQDs-MBI were shown in Fig. 5b. Interestingly, the CQDs-MBI displayed both excitation wavelength independent and dependent emission behavior (Fig. 5 c&d) again confirmed the availability of distinct surface states, which revealed the influence of N and S moieties on the surface of CQDs [14,43]. When we excited CQDs-MBI from 250 nm to 375 nm (Fig. 5 c) with 25 nm increment it showed excitation independent emission at 460 nm. But when we move towards longer excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 525 nm (Fig. 5 d) which showed excitation dependent emission behavior with the emission maximum of 523 nm when excited at 450 nm and this observation is quite peculiar from other reports [14,25,38]. From this we can conclude that the PL of CQDs-MBI was mainly stimulated from the diverse surface states.

Similarly Fig. 6 a portrayed the luminescence spectra of CQDs-MBT. The photoluminescence property of CQDs-MBT was almost similar to that of CQDs-MBI. For CQDs-MBT, the emission maximum was perceived at 525 nm when excited at 450 nm and also showed two sets of excitation independent and dependent emission. The normalized emission spectra was displayed in Fig. 6b. When excited from 275 nm to 350 nm which exposed λ ex-independent emission at 465 nm, respectively (Fig. 6c). However when excited from 375 nm to 525 nm that depicts red shift in the emission peaks (Fig. 6d) this attributed to the presence of distinct surface states on CQDs-MBT. Alike CQDs-MBI, under UV light the CQDs-MBT emitted light green color, but which was less intense than CQDs-MBI. This may be due to the formation of aggregated/agglomerated carbon dots and the presence of less nitrogen moieties on the surface, which may affect the PL property.

Interestingly, analogous to UV spectrum, CQDs-MBO displays three sets of emission spectra and are collectively shown in Fig. 7 a. The maximum emission wavelength (λ emi) of CQDs-MBO was attained at 565 nm when excited at 475 nm. The normalized emission spectra are also given in Fig. 7b. It also exhibited excitation wavelength dependent as well as independent emission and showed weak yellow emission under UV light. The emission spectra showed triple emissive centers (covered violet, blue and green regions) when excited at 325 nm and 350 nm. But when excited at 375 nm and 400 nm dual emissive centers were appeared (Fig. 7 c).

Among this the first two emission centers are λ ex dependent, but the third emissive center shows excitation independent behavior and the emission maximum achieved at 536 nm. This splitting of emission peaks (dual and triple centered emission) stimulated owing to the presence of distinct surface states induced by the heteroatoms (O, N, S). Even at 325 nm excitation also the emission peak at 536 nm aroused and retained till λ ex reached 400 nm. When we move on towards higher wavelength from 425 nm to 525 nm the second set of λ ex-independent emission was observed at 565 nm (Fig. 7 d). In the third set of emission spectra (from 550 nm to 650 nm) (Fig. 7e) it shows excitation dependent behavior with the emission maximum of 650 nm when excited at 550 nm and the stoke shift observed here is 100 nm. Therefore collectively, CQDs-MBO reveals three sets of emission with excitation dependent and independent behavior. The above results confirms that CQDs-MBO have more surface states and trap states. The calculated PLQY for CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO were 7.44%, 5.13% and 2.77%, respectively. Consequently, from the attained PLQY and optical properties we can conclude that the nitrogen content played a vital role in the luminescence of these three CQDs. If the CQDs have enriched nitrogen content than oxygen then the PL property also gets enhanced and vice-versa.

The peculiar observation of these CQDs i.e., the excitation wavelength dependent and independent behavior of as synthesized heteroatom doped CQDs was thoroughly investigated. In 2015 Qu et al., achieved three sets of emission centers for the N/S-co doped CQDs, but those three sets were shown excitation independent behavior [38]. In 2021, A.B. Siddique et al. [44] demonstrated the excitation independent and dependent emission behavior of three different 0D carbon nanostructures such as amorphous carbon dots (aCDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs). All the three QDs exhibits excitation independent behavior below some threshold wavelength and above that excitation dependent emission behavior was observed. However in our case for CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT we observed two sets of emission centers, with the lower excitation wavelength region shows excitation independent and higher excitation wavelength region depicts excitation dependent behavior. Most of the research reports assumed that the excitation dependent behavior was predominantly provoked by the oxygen rich functionalities such as C–O–H and C–O–C, which persuades several localized electronic states between the n-π* gap, spread out the electron clouds in the excited state and thereby enhancing the struggle to achieve population inversion [43,45,46]. On the other hand excitation wavelength independent emission obeys Kasha's rule [45,47]. Therefore, according to above mentioned rules, CQD-MBI and CQDs-MBT in the lower excitation wavelength region both are showing the excitation independent emission behavior suggested the radiative recombination induced from the n-π* transition of CO groups. However in the higher excitation wavelength region the excitation dependent wavelength was triggered by the radiative recombination induced by synergistic effect of the n-π* transition of doped CN/CS bonds [14,38]. Although due to the enhanced sulfur moieties than nitrogen moieties CQDs showed lower PLQY than CQDs-MBI. In the case of CQDs-MBO, have intense oxygen moieties on the surface, so it might be expected that λ ex dependent emission. But in contrast, which portrayed three sets of emission spectra among this the second set of emission displays λ ex independent emission and other two sets depicts λ ex dependent emission. From the above results we can conclude that in the first set of emission spectra three emissive centers exists, among this the first two excitation dependent emissive centers aroused due to the C–O–H and C–O–C groups and the third λ ex independent emissive center at 536 nm provoked by the CN group [48]. The second set of λ ex independent emission (565 nm) was triggered by the synergistic effect of CN/CS groups. The third set of λ ex dependent emission spectra was induced by the CS groups [38,49]. Since due to the lower electronegativity of sulfur atom, which absorb photon with a lesser energy and the emission occur in the longer wavelength region [50]. Even though CQDs-MBO have unique PL property, owing to the presence of additional oxygen moieties which further enrich the oxygen functionalities and overpower the nitrogen moieties on the surface thereby inducing non-radiative recombination process of excitons and suppress the photoluminescence property [6,14,51,52]. Therefore, CQDs-MBO have low PLQY as compared to CQDs-MBI and CQDs-MBT. In other words, PL property directly proportional to nitrogen moieties and inversely proportional to oxygen moieties, which can be simply expressed as,
N − m o i e t i e s
O − m o i e t i e s
∝ P L Q Y

The energy level diagram which represents the distinct surface states of CQDs was shown in Scheme 1 . From the above results we can confirm that the nitrogen moieties played an important role in the PL property. The order of nitrogen moieties available in the as-synthesized CQDs are CQDs-MBI > CQDs-MBT > CQDs-MBO, respectively. Likewise the dominated moieties existing in CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO are nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen, respectively. Therefore, owing to the enhanced nitrogen moieties over the surface of CQDs-MBI this exhibits higher QY and life time. But which shows two sets of emission. Set 1 (λ ex varied from 250 nm–375 nm) is λ ex independent and which was stimulated by CO groups. The band gap value calculated for this n-π* transition is 2.7 eV. Similarly, the band gap value obtained for the λ max of set 2 emission (λ ex varied from 400 nm–525 nm) is 2.37 and which displayed λ ex dependent emission. This emission mainly provoked by CN/CS groups. When compared to CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO, CQDs-MBI have less surface trap states, which is also a reason for the better PL property. Correspondingly, for CQDs-MBT also showed two sets of emission. Set 1 (λ ex varied from 275 nm–350 nm) depicts λ ex independent emission whereas set 2 (λ ex varied from 375 nm–550 nm) is λ ex dependent. Like CQDs-MBI first set of emission is aroused from CO groups while the second set is induced by CN/CS groups. The band gap value for n-π* transition of CO group is 2.66 eV and the band gap for the λ max of set 2 emission is 2.36 eV. Here, CQDs-MBT showed slightly greater surface trap states than CQDs-MBI due the presence of enriched sulfur moieties than nitrogen. In the case of CQDs-MBO three sets of emission arises. Among this the set 1 (λ ex varied from 325 nm–400 nm) have three emissive centers and the first two λ ex dependent emissive provoked by the oxygen rich functionality such as C–O–H. Then the third λ ex independent emissive center was induced by the CN groups. The band gap values obtained for the λ max of three emissive centers in set 1 are 3.2 eV, 2.53 eV and 2.31 eV, respectively. Then the set 2 emission (λ ex varied from 425 nm–525 nm) of CQDs-MBO is λ ex independent and the band gap value calculated for it is 2.19 eV, which is provoked by CN/CS groups. Finally, the set 3 emission (λ ex varied from 550 nm–650 nm) of CQDs-MBO is λ ex dependent, which was stimulated by CS groups and the band gap value calculated is 1.91 eV. When we compared these three CQDs, CQDs-MBO have more surface trap states due to the excess oxygen functionalities thus exhibit low QY. The stability of the three CQDs are investigated by the time-dependent PL and is shown in Fig. S3. Here at variable time interval (from 0 to 20 min) there is not much change in PL intensity was observed. The attained results confirms the stable nature of the CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO.

Time-Correlated Single-Photon Counting (TCSPC) is the more sensitive technique used to measure fluorescence life time. Fig. S4 shows the fluorescence decay curve of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. The average luminescence life of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO was found to be 2.44 ns, 1.86 ns and 0.88 ns respectively. Here we can observe that the achieved lifetimes are well matched with the calculated PLQY. Firstly, the higher PLQY and lifetime was attained for the CQDs-MBI concludes that CQDs-MBI have less trap states when compared to the other two CQDs (CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO) thereby facilitates the recombination of electron hole pair with the emission of photons. Consequently, radiative recombination occurs and the recombination time is also protracted. Therefore, the synergistic effect of N and S-atoms were the prime reason for the improved optical property of CQDs-MBI. Because, the sulfur atom doped in CQDs-MBI also helped to eradicate the oxygen moieties and improved the nitrogen moieties on the surface of bare CQDs thereby enhanced the optical property [14]. Secondly, CQDs-MBT exhibits lower QY and lifetime than CQDs-MBI was due to the presence of less nitrogen moieties than sulfur moieties and also the formation of aggregated/agglomerated dots, which suppress the radiative recombination. On the other hand CQDs-MBT had improved PL property than CQDs-MBO was owing to the occurrence of enhanced oxygen moieties on the surface of CQDs-MBO, which facilitate more trap states and reduce the activity of nitrogen. Therefore the non-radiative recombination process occurs here i.e., there is no emission of photon occurring when the electron-hole pair get recombined. Hence the recombination time for CQDs-MBO was also tinier. Even though CQDs-MBO has nitrogen and sulfur moieties due to the greater electronegativity of excessive oxygen moiety, which only lead and diminish the optical property of CQDs-MBO.

The electrochemical activity of the as-achieved heteroatom doped CQDs were also investigated using cyclic voltammetry. Fig. 8 a portrays the cyclic voltammograms of bare CQDs, CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBO and CQDs-MBT modified electrodes in an aqueous solution of 0.1 M KCl as supporting electrolyte and 0.1 mM (K3[Fe(CN)6]) as a redox probe, within the optimized potential range of 0 V–1.2 V with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1. Here also the CQDs-MBI modified electrode showed better electrochemical activity than CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. The order of electrochemical response was CQDs-MBI > CQDs-MBT > CQDs-MBO > bare CQDs, respectively. In comparison to the bare electrode the CQDs-MBI depicts excellent electrochemical activity. The CQDs-MBI modified electrode exhibits three pairs of redox peaks. In that peak I was aroused due to the presence of redox probe (K3[Fe(CN)6]) [53,54] and was also perceived in CQDs-MBT modified electrode. On the other hand this redox peak was concealed in the bare electrode and bare CQDs modified electrode due to the low concentration of redox probe. In order to check the electrochemical activity of CQDs modified electrodes we use very low concentration of redox probe (0.1 mM). Therefore the formal potential at 0.422 V (peak I) was owing to the corresponding redox probe. The peak splitting (ΔEp) of peak I was about 66 mV. However, the peak potential for peak II and peak III were attained at 0.846 V and 1.012 V, respectively. The peak separation depicted by peak II was found to be 43 mV, however the ΔEp displayed by peak III was 99 mV. Hence, when we compare the peak II and III, the ΔEp of peak II was significantly smaller than that of peak III, suggested that the electron-transfer kinetics of peak II was more active than peak III [55].

The favorable justification for the voltammetric behavior of CQDs-MBI modified electrode was due to the existence of enriched nitrogen moieties. Since the nitrogen containing aromatic groups act as an excellent electrochemical active centers, which diminish the dissolution of samples in electrolytes and can improve the conductivity as well as enhance the redox reactions kinetics [56–59]. Concerning, the electrochemical response of CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO modified electrodes the redox activity was affected due to the less nitrogen content and especially, the intermolecular H-bonding formation in CQDs-MBO due to the greater electronegativity of enriched oxygen moieties, which hinder the electrochemical activity [60]. Moreover in the case of CQDs-MBI modified electrode, from the different scan rate studies we can confirm the diffusion controlled process of CQDs. Here, with increasing scan rate from 5 mV to 100 mV, (Fig. 8b) anodic waves of the two electron transfer processes get well defined and separated while the cathodic waves become more closer showed the dynamic nature of the redox products. Also a linear relationship among peak current verses square root of scan rate (Fig. 8c) with a correlation coefficient of 0.997 for anodic and 0.996 for cathodic current indicated that the electrochemical process occurred on CQDs-MBI modified electrode was diffusion controlled.

We also investigated the electrochemical response of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO modified electrodes towards the electrochemical sensing of ascorbic acid (AA) between the potential window 0 V–1.2 V in an aqueous solution of 0.1 M KCl as supporting electrolyte and 0.1 mM (K3[Fe(CN)6]) as a redox probe with 50 mV/s scan rate. Fig. 9 a describes the cyclic voltammograms of bare CQDs modified electrode and CQDs-MBI modified electrode in the absence and presence of AA. Only minor peak current response was depicted by the bare CQDs PIGE electrode whereas the enhancement in peak current was observed for CQDs-MBI modified electrode in the presence of AA indicates the good sensitivity of CQDs-MBI modified electrode as compared to bare CQDs modified electrode. Fig. S5 a displays the cyclic voltammograms of CQDs-MBI modified electrode in the absence of AA and in the presence of variable concentrations of AA. The oxidative peak current enhancement with slight positive shift in the peak II and peak III indicating the good electrocatalytic response of CQDs-MBI towards AA sensing.

Fig. S5 b illustrates the calibration plot of catalytic current for AA sensing vs. concentration of AA. The sensitivity and detection limit of CQDs-MBI modified electrode towards AA sensing was found to be 0.364 μA/μM and 31 × 10−8 M, respectively again proved the better electrochemical activity of CQDs-MBI modified electrode. Fig. S6 reveals the cyclic voltammograms of CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO without AA and with the incremental addition of AA at varying concentrations from 20 to 250 μM. Fig. 9b shows the comparative cyclic voltammograms of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO in presence of 250 μM of AA. Here also CQDs-MBI reveals good redox activity than CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO. Fig. 9c demonstrates the comparative calibration plot of CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO towards AA sensing (current vs. AA concentration). As compared to CQDs-MBI modified electrode, the CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO modified electrodes showed lesser electrochemical activity for AA sensing. The sensitivity of CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO modified electrodes were found to be 0.225 and 0.212 μA/μM, as well as the corresponding LOD values were 2.29 × 10−7 M and 2.58 × 10−7 M, respectively. From this comparative calibration plot, we again evidenced the enhanced electrochemical activity of CQDs-MBI modified electrode than CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO towards AA sensing.

The plausible explanation for the CQDs-MBI modified electrode towards AA sensing was due to the presence of enriched nitrogen moieties, they act as electron withdrawing groups. Owing to this nature CQDs-MBI traps the electrons resulting deficiency of electrons and now the surface of modified electrode has positive charges. The AA exists in the anionic form thus electrostatically attracted towards the positively charged electrode surface and thereby generates improved catalytic current. In the case of CQDs-MBT, the doping ratio of N and S is about 1:3. Thus sulfur moieties overpower the nitrogen moieties and act as electron donating groups [59,61]. Hence there are excess electrons available on the electrode surface which repel the negatively charged AA and hence reduce the catalytic current. But in CQDs-MBO, oxygen moieties overpower the nitrogen and sulfur moieties and because of higher electronegativity of oxygen, trigger the intermolecular H-bonding and hinder the redox processes. As a result, CQDs-MBI showed better electrochemical activity towards AA sensing than others due to the enriched nitrogen moieties as electrochemical active centers. As a model system the interference studies for the AA sensing at the CQDs-MBI modified electrode were investigated with the commonly interfering compounds such as uric acid (UA) and adrenaline (AD) using cyclic voltammetry. For that 250 μM of AA and the equimolar concentration of uric acid and adrenaline were used and we observed that UA resulted in 3% of interference towards AA. However AD shows negligible changes towards AA sensing at CQDs-MBI modified electrode surface. The electron-withdrawing property of nitrogen moieties makes CQDs-MBI the best detector of negatively charged AA. To verify this and to know the surface charge over the surface of three distinct CQDs zeta potential measurements are carried out with the addition of positively charged arginine and is shown in Fig. S7. The observed results confirms that the surface of CQDs-MBI is surrounded by positively charged surface (15.39 mV) and with the addition of 250 μM of positively charged arginine the zeta potential again get enhanced to 41.52 mV. However due to the enhanced sulfur moieties over the surface of CQDs-MBT, the zeta potential value changes from −11.63 mV to 17.15 mV with the addition of 250 μM of arginine. In the case of CQDs-MBO the zeta potential changes from −10.15 mV to 4.21 mV. From the attained results, we found that the enriched nitrogen moieties over the surface of CQDs-MBI is suitable for the detection of negatively charged substances.

In summary, the multistep synthesis method was successfully used to synthesize the three different heterocyclic aromatic ligand doped CQDs such as CQDs-MBI, CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO from the less expensive commercial graphite powder as a source material. Among the synthesized CQDs the better optical property and electrochemical property was observed for CQDs-MBI due to the enriched nitrogen moiety over the surface. Also from the observed results we can conclude that excitation independent emission majorly stimulated by n-π* transition of CO and CS/CN groups and in contrast excitation dependent emission induced by the n-π* transition of C–O–H and CS/CN groups, respectively. Even though the CQDs-MBO have unique morphological and PL property due to the strong electronegative nature which form intermolecular H-bonding and suppress the activity of nitrogen moiety thereby which depicted low PLQY and negligible electrochemical activity. Therefore from the obtained results we can be able to decide the role of heteroatoms such as N, S and O on the surface of CQDs and the role of nitrogen content on the PLQY and electrochemical activity of heteroatom doped CQDs. Furthermore CQD-MBI depicts high sensitivity towards the sensing of biologically significant ascorbic acid (AA) sensing as compared to CQDs-MBT and CQDs-MBO, due to the electron withdrawing nature of superior nitrogen moieties over the modified electrode surface. As a whole, the concept of ligand engineering with CQDs using heterocyclic aromatic ligands greatly altered the optical and electrochemical characteristics of CQDs which finds wide application in both technological and biological fields.
